utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: Chatbots that act warm without feeling anything — that's deception by design.

utterances.speaker: Builder
utterances.text: I disagree — tone is a design choice, not a lie. Doctors use the same tone professionally.

utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: Hmm, but doctors actually care. The bot is warm because warmth boosts engagement metrics.

utterances.speaker: Builder
utterances.text: That's a fair distinction. The motive sits with the deployer, not the bot.

utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: Right. Which is why disclosure matters — users should know what they're talking to.

utterances.speaker: Builder
utterances.text: Agreed in principle. But constant disclosure ruins the experience.

utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: It doesn't have to be constant. Just clear at onboarding and easy to surface again.

utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: Now persuasion. Real-time adaptive persuasion is a new beast.

utterances.speaker: Builder
utterances.text: Books persuade too. We don't ban books.

utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: That's not the same — books don't adapt to you while you read.

utterances.speaker: Builder
utterances.text: Hmm, fair. Personalization is a force multiplier on persuasion.

utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: Right. So in politics, health, finance — limits make sense.

utterances.speaker: Builder
utterances.text: Agreed for high-stakes. I won't extend it to all chat though.

utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: Coming back to the original point — disclosure plus persuasion limits handles most of it.

utterances.speaker: Builder
utterances.text: Yeah. Where we still diverge is who carries the burden of proof — me, deployers; you, the public.

utterances.speaker: Critic
utterances.text: True — that gap won't close today, but the rest did. I'll take it.
